mnemonics: Quartered Pizza: Imagine a pizza cut into four quarters. The first quarter (25th percentile) and third quarter (75th percentile) mark the delicious crust edges of the middle half, which is the IQR.
Order, Locate, Interpolate: Remember the three steps for finding a percentile: 1) Order the data, 2) Locate the position, 3) Interpolate if necessary.
IQR Fences: Think of the IQR as a fence. Outliers are the sheep that jump over the top fence (Q3 + 1.5*IQR) or under the bottom fence (Q1 - 1.5*IQR).
keyPoints: The 25th percentile (Q1) is the value below which 25% of the data falls.
The 75th percentile (Q3) is the value below which 75% of the data falls.
The Interquartile Range (IQR) is calculated as IQR = Q3 - Q1 and represents the spread of the middle 50% of the data, making it a robust measure of variability that is resistant to outliers.
commonMistakes: Confusing the percentile calculation formula. A common method is L = (P/100)*n, but this can yield a decimal position, leading to confusion between using the value at that position or interpolating.
Forgetting to order the dataset before attempting to find the percentiles, which will yield an incorrect result.
Mistaking the IQR for the full range of the data (max - min), when it only covers the central portion.
